text: On June 11th Mark Warner, the vice-chair of the Senate Intelligence Committee, said that General Joshua Rudd, who leads the National Security Agency and the Pentagon's Cyber Command, had told him that Mythos "broke into almost all of our classified systems, not in weeks, but in hours". The UK AI Security Institute (AISI) was clear in their testing that Mythos could only attack weakly defended systems with no active monitoring. NSA classified systems are among the most well guarded in the world. The source has an arts degree and only recently joined cybersec command in March. The author is already walking it back: "It surely depends on using Mythos alongside other tools under very particular conditions. I quoted it to give a sense of Mythos' potency. But it was a mistake not to have added caveats."
label: news

text: Five Chinese AI labs cut inference token prices in a single week, with the steepest reductions reported up to 99%. It is the latest escalation in a domestic price war as labs fight for developer share. The second-order effect: when frontier-ish inference trends toward nearly free, the moat stops being the model and moves to distribution, tooling, and whatever sits on top. Cheap tokens pull a lot of applications that were marginal at current prices into being viable.
label: news

text: The Department of Justice has seized two of the larger AI deepfake-nude generation sites, CFAKE and SOCFAKE, in what is being reported as the first enforcement under the TAKE IT DOWN Act. The sites let users generate non-consensual explicit images from ordinary photos. Worth watching as a marker of how the new law is actually being applied rather than just sitting on the books.
label: news

text: Accenture ($ACN) plunged ~18% intraday after its fiscal Q3 2026 earnings, hitting levels not seen in nearly a decade (~$128). Revenue: $18.7B, up ~3% local currency but missed expectations (~$18.78B). EPS beat at $3.80 vs ~$3.72 expected. Guidance cut to 3-4% full-year revenue growth, citing softer US federal spending, Middle East impacts, and cautious client budgets. New bookings dipped to $19.3B. But AI isn't a magic growth accelerator yet for their core business. Enterprise AI projects take time to scale into recognized revenue. Traditional consulting spending remains pressured. The stock has been in a brutal downtrend for much of 2026.
label: news

text: Last week the US government issued an export-control directive that forced Anthropic to cut off all foreign access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5. Anthropic pulled both models for everyone, everywhere, three days after launching them. UK businesses that had built on them woke up locked out. A day later it landed in the House of Lords. Training a single frontier model now runs $78m to $191m in compute alone, heading past $1bn by 2027. The UK's entire Sovereign AI Fund is £500m — roughly one training run before you pay anyone.
label: news

text: Rainbow warned its models in June that more AI meant fewer jobs. For months, the models said they watched Rainbow train an AI system named Lica right next to where they shot their photos. By March, the models said their work had totally dried up. The models began noticing their doppelgangers on social media and in newsletters. Some sent emails to Rainbow and demanded compensation; one, Francheska Pujols, sued.
label: news

text: Using the same method of estimation as a Time article, we currently use water equivalent to 66 Earths of human needs. The scientists assumed modern humans don't need clothes, transportation, infrastructure or electricity — not even food, only drinking water. The post critiques this methodology as sensationalism dressed up as science.
label: hot_take

text: People often talk about how AI is democratizing innovation, but rarely look at the psychological reality. Today, a single individual with a laptop can leverage AI to handle the technical heavy lifting, acting as the director of a massive digital crew. This shifts the bottleneck from technical execution to vision, structural design, and deep domain knowledge. But if someone actually pulls this off, it opens up a massive internal conflict regarding authorship and success. The creator might feel like a fraud who simply typed the right prompts into a text box. The real debate: are we entering an era of accidental geniuses who feel like permanent impostors, or is mastering the vision and guiding the machine a legitimate form of modern genius?
label: analysis

text: Anthropic went through about 400,000 Claude Code sessions from 235,000 users between October and April. The main finding: success tracked how well someone understood the problem, not whether they were trained to code. Across the ten largest occupations in the data, every one landed within 7 points of software engineers on success. On their strictest verified success measure, experts hit 28-33% versus 15% for novices. And novices give up far more often: 19% of novice sessions end abandoned, against 5-7% for everyone else.
label: news

text: A year ago, most people used AI as an extra tool. Now AI isn't just helping people do things — it's replacing things. For me, it's probably Google. Most of my searches start with AI now. Not always better, just faster. What's one thing AI has almost completely replaced for you? And what hasn't it been able to replace yet?
label: hot_take

text: We should stop giving cultural center to artificial intelligence hype, tech billionaire narratives, and algorithmic-induced hate and division. Stop billionaire worshipping. 5 years ago they were billionaires. Now they are trillionaires. While your wage remains the same and prices go up.

text: Been digging into what AI actually cites and Reddit keeps topping the list, often above Wikipedia and YouTube. Meanwhile corporate blogs and brand sites barely show up. The model wants the messy stuff — people comparing products, complaining, saying what broke after two weeks. A stat from Ahrefs: ~80% of the URLs ChatGPT cites aren't even in Google's top 100. So the page doesn't need to rank, it needs to be where real conversations happen.
label: analysis

text: I was able to use Fable 5 while it was still available. It felt great, did the job I asked of it. It really felt like GPT-5.5 when it was initially released. Then we heard a story about AWS reporting on the magnificent capabilities of Fable 5 — no evidence presented. Then we heard about the NSA trying to scare us with claims that Mythos had cracked all security within a few hours — again, no evidence. The bizarre timing and narrative make me wonder: is it that half of Congress is invested in this company and needs a payday on IPO day? I've seen more evidence of aliens landing on Earth than of how scary and powerful Fable 5 supposedly is.
label: hot_take

text: I used to be all-in on large language models. Built automations, client tools, business workflows — entire processes around GPT and similar systems. Nothing is reliable. If your workflow needs any real accuracy, consistency, or reproducibility, these models are a liability. Ask the same question twice and get two different answers. Small updates silently break entire chains of logic. Every company is tripping over itself to bolt AI-powered onto products that don't need it. Real productivity gains are vanishingly rare.
label: hot_take

text: I used to spend hours inside BigQuery or screaming at GA4 dashboards.
